draft.introduction: The Rígsþula (“List of Rígr”) narrates how the god Heimdallr, traveling as Rígr, visits three households and fathers the ancestors of the three social classes: thralls, karls, and jarls. It is the Norse origin myth for the social order.

translation.text: In ancient times, they say, along green paths went a wise god, mighty and ancient, strong and vigorous, Rígr striding. He went further along the middle of the road and came to a dwelling. The door was ajar; he entered. Fire was on the floor, and a grey-haired couple sat there.
translation.original_text: Ár kváðu ganga grœnar brautir ǫlgan ok aldinn, ás kunnigan, ramman ok rǭskan, Ríg stíganda.

translator_notes.note: The Rígsþula is the only Norse poem that explicitly provides a divine origin for social hierarchy. Its structure closely parallels the Vedic Purusha Sukta (RV 10.90), where the four varnas emerge from the cosmic man’s body, suggesting a shared Indo-European ideology of sacred social stratification.
